sectionName: Biological & Biochemical Foundations of Living Systems

questions.passage: Researchers compare otherwise similar human cells with functional p53 (WT) or deleted p53 (p53-null). After ionizing radiation, WT cells increase p53 and p21 expression. Twenty-four hours later, cells were stained with propidium iodide (PI) and analyzed via flow cytometry for DNA content. The cell count distributions are shown in Figure 1. BrdU incorporation assays confirm that irradiated WT cells show sharply reduced DNA synthesis relative to p53-null cells.
questions.stem: The radiation-induced 2N accumulation in WT cells most directly indicates arrest in:

explanations.A: G₂ cells have completed DNA replication and therefore contain 4N DNA. They contribute to the higher-DNA peak rather than the 2N accumulation described. Flow-cytometric DNA content links peaks to cell-cycle position: G₁ cells have 2N DNA, S-phase cells lie between peaks, and G₂/M cells have 4N DNA before cytokinesis.
explanations.B: Correct. Diploid cells in G₁ contain 2N DNA because replication has not begun. p53-dependent p21 induction blocks G₁/S progression, so damaged WT cells remain in the 2N peak instead of entering S phase. Flow-cytometric DNA content links peaks to cell-cycle position: G₁ cells have 2N DNA, S-phase cells lie between peaks, and G₂/M cells have 4N DNA before cytokinesis.
explanations.C: M-phase cells also contain 4N DNA until chromosome segregation and cytokinesis are completed. DNA content alone cannot distinguish G₂ from M, but it excludes both from the 2N peak. Flow-cytometric DNA content links peaks to cell-cycle position: G₁ cells have 2N DNA, S-phase cells lie between peaks, and G₂/M cells have 4N DNA before cytokinesis.
explanations.D: During S phase DNA content lies between 2N and 4N as replication proceeds. Late S-phase cells approach 4N rather than accumulating sharply at 2N. Flow-cytometric DNA content links peaks to cell-cycle position: G₁ cells have 2N DNA, S-phase cells lie between peaks, and G₂/M cells have 4N DNA before cytokinesis.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 1. DNA content analysis by flow cytometry after ionizing radiation

questions.stem: Which sequence best describes the p53-p21 checkpoint mechanism?

choices.label: A
choices.text: p53 induces p21, p21 inhibits cyclin-CDK activity, and hypophosphorylated Rb restrains E2F

choices.label: B
choices.text: p21 degrades p53, causing Rb phosphorylation and arrest

choices.label: C
choices.text: Rb activates CDKs, which inhibit E2F

choices.label: D
choices.text: E2F induces p21 to accelerate S phase

explanations.A: Correct. Damage-stabilized p53 increases p21 transcription. p21 suppresses cyclin-CDK complexes, so Rb remains hypophosphorylated and bound to E2F, preventing transcription of S-phase genes. This creates a reversible arrest while repair occurs. p53 is a transcription factor activated by cellular stress, whereas p21 is a downstream CDK inhibitor. Distinguishing regulator from effector prevents reversal of the checkpoint pathway.
explanations.B: p21 is induced by p53 rather than serving primarily to degrade it. Increased Rb phosphorylation would release E2F and favor, not block, S-phase entry. p53 is a transcription factor activated by cellular stress, whereas p21 is a downstream CDK inhibitor.
explanations.C: Cyclin-CDK complexes phosphorylate Rb; Rb does not activate those kinases. Hypophosphorylated Rb restrains E2F-dependent transcription. p53 is a transcription factor activated by cellular stress, whereas p21 is a downstream CDK inhibitor.
explanations.D: E2F promotes genes required for DNA synthesis. p21 inhibits progression and does not accelerate S phase in the pathway described. p53 is a transcription factor activated by cellular stress, whereas p21 is a downstream CDK inhibitor.

questions.stem: Why is BrdU incorporation reduced in irradiated WT cells?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Radiation removes all thymidine from the medium

choices.label: D
choices.text: Fewer WT cells synthesize DNA after checkpoint activation

explanations.A: BrdU is a thymidine analog incorporated into newly synthesized nuclear DNA, not a probe of oxygen consumption or mitochondrial electron transport. BrdU incorporation measures active DNA synthesis.
explanations.B: The experiment does not indicate depletion of thymidine. BrdU availability is shared across groups, so differential incorporation reflects cell behavior. BrdU incorporation measures active DNA synthesis.
explanations.C: Checkpoint arrest does not halve chromosome sets. WT cells remain diploid with 2N DNA in G₁. BrdU incorporation measures active DNA synthesis.
explanations.D: Correct. p53-p21 signaling prevents many damaged WT cells from crossing G₁/S, so fewer cells replicate DNA and incorporate BrdU. Persistent BrdU incorporation in p53-null cells shows continued synthesis despite damage. BrdU incorporation measures active DNA synthesis. A treatment-induced decrease therefore reports reduced S-phase entry or progression rather than directly measuring apoptosis or chromosome number.

questions.stem: The p53 deletion in these cells is most consistent with loss of a:

choices.label: C
choices.text: Tumor suppressor that normally limits damaged-cell proliferation

explanations.A: Proto-oncogenes promote growth when activated; their loss would not typically remove a checkpoint. p53 acts oppositely by restraining propagation of damaged genomes. A tumor suppressor generally restrains proliferation and is commonly disabled by loss-of-function events; an oncogene generally promotes growth and is activated by gain-of-function or overexpression.
explanations.B: Some receptors are oncogenic when overactive, but the passage describes a nuclear stress-response regulator and transcription factor rather than a membrane receptor. A tumor suppressor generally restrains proliferation and is commonly disabled by loss-of-function events; an oncogene generally promotes growth and is activated by gain-of-function or overexpression.
explanations.C: Correct. p53 is a tumor suppressor: it promotes arrest, repair, senescence, or apoptosis after stress. Loss-of-function allows damaged cells to continue replicating, increasing mutation accumulation and cancer risk. A tumor suppressor generally restrains proliferation and is commonly disabled by loss-of-function events; an oncogene generally promotes growth and is activated by gain-of-function or overexpression.
explanations.D: A constitutively active cyclin would be a gain-of-function growth-promoting lesion. The experimental manipulation is deletion of a negative regulator. A tumor suppressor generally restrains proliferation and is commonly disabled by loss-of-function events; an oncogene generally promotes growth and is activated by gain-of-function or overexpression.

questions.stem: Why can persistent damage produce apoptosis rather than indefinite arrest?

choices.label: B
choices.text: p53 can induce proapoptotic targets such as BAX when damage is irreparable

explanations.A: Arrest can be temporary or lead to senescence without membrane rupture. Apoptosis requires regulated signaling rather than automatic lysis. Checkpoint arrest is protective only when repair is possible.
explanations.B: Correct. p53 output depends on damage magnitude and duration. When repair is unlikely, transcription of BAX, PUMA, and related genes promotes mitochondrial apoptosis, preventing a severely damaged genome from reentering the proliferative pool. Checkpoint arrest is protective only when repair is possible. Persistent damage can shift p53 output toward proapoptotic genes, linking genome surveillance to elimination of irreparably damaged cells.
explanations.C: p21 is a CDK inhibitor, not a nuclease. It helps establish arrest but does not directly fragment DNA. Checkpoint arrest is protective only when repair is possible.
explanations.D: Rb regulates E2F in the nucleus and does not become an extracellular death-receptor ligand. Checkpoint arrest is protective only when repair is possible.

questions.passage:
A research group tests Drug M in lymphoma cells. Within two hours, Bax accumulates at mitochondria, cytochrome c appears in the cytosol, caspase-9 is activated, and executioner caspase-3 cleaves cellular substrates. Cells engineered to overexpress Bcl-2 show little cytochrome-c release and survive substantially longer. Vehicle-treated cells show minimal caspase activation, supporting the conclusion that the observed sequence is Drug M dependent.

In a second experiment, the broad caspase inhibitor z-VAD-fmk prevents caspase-3 substrate cleavage but does not prevent Bax translocation or cytochrome-c release. Early after Drug M treatment, many cells bind fluorescent Annexin V but exclude propidium iodide (PI). At later times, many cells become positive for both probes. Annexin V binds phosphatidylserine exposed on the outer plasma-membrane leaflet, whereas PI enters cells only after substantial loss of membrane integrity.

The investigators propose combining Drug M with a treatment that restores p53 activity in tumors retaining an intact but suppressed p53 gene.
questions.stem: Drug M most directly activates which apoptotic pathway?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Extrinsic apoptosis initiated only by a death receptor

explanations.A: Membrane rupture is not the initiating event; early cells exclude PI. The ordered Bax, cytochrome-c, and caspase sequence supports regulated apoptosis. The intrinsic apoptotic pathway is organized around mitochondrial outer-membrane permeabilization, cytochrome-c release, apoptosome formation, initiator caspase-9, and executioner caspases.
explanations.B: Death receptors activate caspase-8 in the canonical extrinsic pathway. The passage instead begins with mitochondrial Bax and cytochrome-c release. The intrinsic apoptotic pathway is organized around mitochondrial outer-membrane permeabilization, cytochrome-c release, apoptosome formation, initiator caspase-9, and executioner caspases.
explanations.C: Autophagy uses lysosomal degradation and does not explain apoptosome-associated caspase-9 followed by caspase-3 activation. The intrinsic apoptotic pathway is organized around mitochondrial outer-membrane permeabilization, cytochrome-c release, apoptosome formation, initiator caspase-9, and executioner caspases.
explanations.D: Correct. Bax-mediated mitochondrial permeabilization releases cytochrome c, enabling apoptosome formation and caspase-9 activation. Caspase-9 then activates executioner caspases such as caspase-3. The intrinsic apoptotic pathway is organized around mitochondrial outer-membrane permeabilization, cytochrome-c release, apoptosome formation, initiator caspase-9, and executioner caspases.

questions.stem: Why does Bcl-2 overexpression reduce Drug M-induced death?

explanations.A: Direct caspase-3 activation would promote rather than prevent apoptosis. Bcl-2 acts upstream at mitochondria. Bcl-2 family proteins regulate the mitochondrial commitment step.
explanations.B: Correct. Antiapoptotic Bcl-2 sequesters or opposes proapoptotic family members, preserving mitochondrial outer-membrane integrity. Reduced cytochrome-c release prevents efficient apoptosome and caspase-9 activation. Bcl-2 family proteins regulate the mitochondrial commitment step. Antiapoptotic members restrain Bax/Bak, whereas proapoptotic members promote membrane permeabilization.
explanations.C: Bcl-2 is not a DNA-damaging nuclease. Its oncogenic survival effect comes from raising the threshold for mitochondrial apoptosis. Bcl-2 family proteins regulate the mitochondrial commitment step.
explanations.D: Phosphatidylserine exposure is an early apoptotic membrane change. Bcl-2 delays that outcome rather than directly causing it. Bcl-2 family proteins regulate the mitochondrial commitment step.

questions.stem: What does the z-VAD-fmk experiment establish about event order?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Bax translocation and cytochrome-c release occur upstream of caspase execution

choices.label: C
choices.text: Cytochrome c is released only after membrane lysis

choices.label: D
choices.text: Caspases are required for the initial mitochondrial response

explanations.A: Correct. Caspase blockade prevents downstream substrate cleavage but leaves Bax movement and cytochrome-c release intact. Events that persist when caspases are inhibited must occur upstream or independently of the blocked executioner step. A downstream caspase inhibitor and an upstream mitochondrial inhibitor produce different molecular signatures. Ordering events requires asking which changes still occur when each step is blocked.
explanations.B: If caspase-3 caused Bax translocation, inhibiting caspases should prevent Bax movement. The observed persistence contradicts this order. A downstream caspase inhibitor and an upstream mitochondrial inhibitor produce different molecular signatures.
explanations.C: Cytochrome c appears before late PI permeability and persists with caspase inhibition, so it is not merely leakage after nonspecific lysis. A downstream caspase inhibitor and an upstream mitochondrial inhibitor produce different molecular signatures.
explanations.D: The initial mitochondrial events occur despite z-VAD-fmk. Caspases are important downstream amplifiers and executioners, not required for the Bax-driven commitment shown. A downstream caspase inhibitor and an upstream mitochondrial inhibitor produce different molecular signatures.

questions.stem: Annexin V-positive, PI-negative cells are best classified as:

choices.label: A
choices.text: Healthy cells with no membrane changes

choices.label: B
choices.text: Necrotic cells with immediate membrane rupture

choices.label: C
choices.text: Early apoptotic cells with phosphatidylserine exposure but preserved membrane integrity

explanations.A: Healthy cells retain phosphatidylserine predominantly on the inner leaflet and therefore show little Annexin V binding. Annexin V binds phosphatidylserine exposed early on the outer membrane; propidium iodide enters only after membrane integrity is lost.
explanations.B: Primary membrane rupture permits PI entry, producing PI positivity. Exclusion of PI argues that gross membrane integrity remains intact. Annexin V binds phosphatidylserine exposed early on the outer membrane; propidium iodide enters only after membrane integrity is lost.
explanations.C: Correct. Early apoptosis externalizes phosphatidylserine, allowing Annexin V binding, while the plasma membrane still excludes PI. Later apoptosis or secondary necrosis produces double-positive cells as integrity is lost. Annexin V binds phosphatidylserine exposed early on the outer membrane; propidium iodide enters only after membrane integrity is lost. Their combination distinguishes early from late death states.
explanations.D: DNA synthesis is assessed by BrdU or cell-cycle DNA content, not by Annexin V/PI staining. Annexin V binds phosphatidylserine exposed early on the outer membrane; propidium iodide enters only after membrane integrity is lost.

questions.topic: Annexin V and PI / Apoptosis Staging

questions.stem: Why might restoring p53 increase sensitivity to Drug M?

choices.label: D
choices.text: Restored p53 can increase proapoptotic signaling while Drug M engages the mitochondrial pathway

explanations.A: p53 selectively regulates many genes; it does not globally shut off transcription. Its restoration can change the balance between repair, arrest, and death. Cancer-cell survival reflects the balance of growth signals, checkpoints, and death pathways.
explanations.B: The passage states that persistent p53 signaling can induce BAX, so p53 restoration would be expected to promote rather than permanently block Bax expression. Cancer-cell survival reflects the balance of growth signals, checkpoints, and death pathways.
explanations.C: Drug M acts in human lymphoma cells and does not depend on bacterial cloning vectors. Cancer-cell survival reflects the balance of growth signals, checkpoints, and death pathways.
explanations.D: Correct. Reactivated p53 can induce Bax and other death-promoting proteins, lowering the threshold for mitochondrial permeabilization. Drug M then drives the same intrinsic pathway, creating a mechanistically coherent combination that may preferentially eliminate damaged tumor cells. Cancer-cell survival reflects the balance of growth signals, checkpoints, and death pathways. Mechanistically complementary treatments can be effective when one restores damage signaling and another lowers the apoptotic threshold.
